name: Footprint quarry 1 1/2 miles SW of Attleboro, MA
short_description: Historic quarry near Attleboro offering opportunities to collect quartz, feldspar, and mica from pegmatitic granite outcrops. Low elevation, easy access site popular with local rockhounders.
description:
Footprint quarry, located about 1.5 miles southwest of Attleboro in Bristol County, is a classic New England pegmatite collecting site. The quarry exposes granite and pegmatite formations typical of the Narragansett Basin geological province, where late-stage hydrothermal processes created coarse-grained mineral assemblages. Collectors here find well-formed quartz crystals, feldspar (including microcline and plagioclase), and abundant mica specimens including muscovite and biotite.

The site sits at only 109 feet elevation in relatively flat terrain, making it accessible to most rockhounders. The quarry walls and exposed rock faces provide excellent views of the pegmatite structure and mineral relationships. Spring and fall offer the most pleasant collecting conditions in southern Massachusetts, though the site is collectible year-round when weather permits.

This location is known locally for producing nice quartz points and feldspar crystals. The quarry environment means specimens are often partially extracted from the host rock, making hand tools sufficient for most collectors. Be prepared for wet conditions during rainy periods, as the quarry floor can become muddy.
history: Footprint quarry developed as a commercial granite operation during the 19th and early 20th centuries, when demand for dimension stone and crushed granite was high in southern New England. The quarrying activity exposed large pegmatitic zones within the Dedham granite formation, which crystallized during the late Paleozoic as part of the Pan-African orogeny. These pegmatites represent the final stages of granite crystallization, when hydrothermal fluids concentrated incompatible elements like silicon, lithium, and beryllium into coarse-grained mineral assemblages.
tips: Focus on the quarry walls and exposed pegmatite faces where you can see the coarsest grain sizes and largest crystals. Bring a geology hammer, cold chisel, and hand lenses to examine mineral textures and identify feldspar varieties by luster and cleavage patterns. Look for quartz crystals in the white and smoky varieties, often associated with pink or flesh-colored feldspars - these pockets are most productive where the pegmatite is thickest. Water seepage from springs often indicates productive mineral zones.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently sloping terrain at 109 feet elevation. The quarry itself has exposed rock faces, open pit floor, and scattered granite blocks. Most of the site is walkable in sturdy footwear, though the quarry floor can be muddy or wet depending on recent rainfall. Vegetation is sparse around active exposures but typical New England deciduous forest surrounds the quarry margins.
written_directions: From downtown Attleboro, take North Main Street (Route 122) southwest toward the Taunton area. Continue for approximately 1.5 miles past the town center. Look for Footprint Road on the left (southeast) side. The quarry is located on or near Footprint Road in the area southwest of Attleboro center. Local knowledge or GPS coordinates (41.944443, -71.28611) are recommended for pinpointing the exact entrance, as signage may be limited.
road_conditions: Paved road to town area, final approach may be unpaved. Road surface conditions vary by season.
parking_notes: Limited informal parking near quarry entrance. Visitors typically park along nearby roads or small pullouts. Capacity and facilities are minimal.
collection_rules: Always obtain permission from property owner before collecting. Personal use collection only. Do not remove specimens for commercial purposes. Stay clear of unstable quarry walls and do not perform excavation that destabilizes rock faces. Respect posted notices and barriers.
rules:
Obtain property owner permission before collecting
Personal use only, no commercial collection
Avoid unstable quarry walls and dangerous drop-offs
Do not perform heavy excavation or blasting
Respect all posted notices and barriers
Fill holes and do not leave hazardous conditions
nearest_services: Attleboro (1.5 miles away) has gas stations, restaurants, and general stores. Town provides full amenities including water sources.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with minimal elevation change. The quarry floor and surrounding area are accessible to most hikers wearing sturdy footwear. No formal wheelchair access, though the flat terrain means some areas could potentially be navigated in wheeled mobility aids with assistance. Wet conditions are common, so appropriate footwear for muddy ground is recommended.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding near Attleboro, Massachusetts?
faq.answer: Footprint quarry offers excellent opportunities to collect quartz (clear, smoky, and milky varieties), feldspar (microcline and plagioclase), and mica (muscovite and biotite sheets). These minerals occur in pegmatitic granite exposures characteristic of the Narragansett Basin region. Most specimens are quartz crystals and feldspar chunks ranging from hand-sized to several inches.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Footprint quarry free or do you need a fee to collect?
faq.answer: Footprint quarry is a public site with no entrance fee for personal use rock and mineral collection. You do not need a permit, but you should obtain permission from the property owner before collecting. This is a historic quarry open to the rockhounding community for casual specimen gathering.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Footprint quarry in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: The best collecting season at Footprint quarry is April through June and September through November, when temperatures are mild and rainfall is moderate. Summer can be hot and humid in southern Massachusetts, while winter weather may limit access. Spring and fall provide comfortable conditions and the quarry is usually accessible during these months.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Footprint quarry for beginners?
faq.answer: Footprint quarry is excellent for beginners. The site sits on flat terrain at only 109 feet elevation with easy walking access to productive quarry faces. Specimens are often partially exposed, so hand tools like geology hammers and cold chisels are sufficient - no heavy equipment needed. Children ages 6 and up can safely participate with adult supervision.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for collecting at Footprint quarry near Attleboro?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, cold chisel or pry bar, hand lens or magnifier, safety glasses, and sturdy hiking boots. The muddy quarry floor and potential for loose rocks make proper footwear essential. A small backpack for specimens and a water bottle complete a basic kit. No power tools or heavy equipment are necessary for this site.

faq.question: Can you take your dog rockhounding at Footprint quarry in Attleboro?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome at Footprint quarry for rockhounding. The flat terrain and open quarry environment are dog-friendly, though you should keep dogs under control and prevent them from running near quarry edges or unstable rock faces. Bring water for your pet and be mindful of the muddy conditions in wet weather.

meta_description: Collect quartz, feldspar, and mica at Footprint quarry near Attleboro. Free public access, easy terrain, beginner-friendly New England pegmatite site.
